The actual status code received\n51\t    - The expected status code(s)\n52\t    - The response body (truncated if too long)\n53\t    \"\"\"\n54\t\n55\t    def __init__(self,\n56\t                 actual: int,\n57\t                 expected: Union[int, List[int]],\n58\t                 response_body: Optional[str] = None,\n59\t                 url: Optional[str] = None):\n60\t        \"\"\"\n61\t        Initialize a status validation error.\n62\t\n63\t        Args:\n64\t            actual: The actual HTTP status code received\n65\t            expected: The expected status code or list of allowed codes\n66\t            response_body: Optional response body for debugging\n67\t            url: Optional URL that was requested\n68\t        \"\"\"\n69\t        self.actual = actual\n70\t        self.expected = expected\n71\t        self.response_body = response_body\n72\t        self.url = url\n73\t\n74\t        # Build detailed error message\n75\t        msg_parts = []\n76\t\n77\t        if url:\n78\t            msg_parts.append(f\"URL: {url}\")\n79\t\n80\t        msg_parts.append(f\"Expected HTTP status code: {self._format_expected()}\")\n81\t        msg_parts.append(f\"Actual status code: {actual}\")\n82\t\n83\t        if response_body:\n84\t            # Truncate response body if too long\n85\t            body_preview = response_body[:200]\n86\t            if len(response_body) > 200:\n87\t                body_preview += \"... (truncated)\"\n88\t            msg_parts.append(f\"Response body: {body_preview}\")\n89\t\n90\t        message = \"\\n  \".join(msg_parts)\n91\t        super().__init__(message)\n92\t\n93\t    def _format_expected(self) -> str:\n94\t        \"\"\"Format expected status codes for display.\"\"\"\n95\t        if isinstance(self.expected, list):\n96\t            if len(self.expected) == 1:\n97\t                return str(self.expected[0])\n98\t            elif len(self.expected) == 2:\n99\t                return f\"{self.expected[0]} or {self.expected[1]}\"\n100\t            else:\n101\t                return f\"one of {self.expected}\"\n102\t        else:\n103\t            return str(self.expected)\n104\t\n105\t\n106\tdef validate_http_status(\n107\t    response: Union[requests.Response, tuple],\n108\t    expected_status: Union[int, List[int]],\n109\t    throw_on_error: bool = True\n110\t) -> bool:\n111\t    \"\"\"\n112\t    Validate HTTP response status code against expected value(s).\n113\t\n114\t    This helper function validates that an HTTP response has the expected status code.\n115\t    It supports both single status codes and lists of allowed codes, making it\n116\t    flexible for different testing scenarios (e.g., accepting both 200 and 204).\n117\t\n118\t    Args:\n119\t        response: HTTP response object (requests.Response) or tuple of otherwise\n126\t\n127\t    Raises:\n128\t        StatusValidationError: If validation fails and throw_on_error is True\n129\t        TypeError: If response or expected_status has invalid type\n130\t\n131\t    Examples:\n132\t        >>> # Single status code validation\n133\t        >>> response = requests.get('http://example.com')\n134\t        >>> validate_http_status(response, validate_http_status(response, 200, throw_on_error=False)\n145\t        >>> if is_valid:\n146\t        ...     print(\"Status code is valid!\")\n147\t\n148\t    Acceptance Criteria:\n149\t    - Function accepts a response object and expected status code(s)\n150\t    - Supports both single status codes and arrays of allowed codes\n151\t    - Returns boolean or throws assertion error with clear message\n152\t    - Includes test cases demonstrating valid/invalid status codes\n153\t    - Function is exported from test utils module\n154\t    \"\"\"\n155\t    # Extract status code and body from response\n156\t    actual_status: int\n157\t    response_body: Optional[str] = None\n158\t    url: Optional[str] = None\n159\t\n160\t    # DESIGN DECISION: Use duck typing for response handling\n161\t    # - Why: Support both requests.Response objects and simple tuples\n162\t    # - Benefit: Tests can work without external dependencies\n163\t    # - Pattern: Check tuple first (special case), then attributes (duck typing)\n164\t\n165\t    # First check if it's a tuple (special case)\n166\t    # Tuples are checked before hasattr() because they don't have status_code attribute\n167\t    # Tuple format: (status_code,) or (status_code, body) or (status_code, headers, body)\n168\t    if isinstance(response, tuple) and len(response) >= 1:\n169\t        actual_status = response[0]\n170\t        if len(response) > 1:\n171\t            response_body = str(response[1])\n172\t    # Then check if it has response-like attributes (status_code, etc.)\n173\t    # This handles requests.Response objects and any similar response-like objects\n174\t    elif hasattr(response, 'status_code'):\n175\t        actual_status = response.status_code\n176\t        response_body = getattr(response, 'text', None)\n177\t        url = getattr(response, 'url', None)\n178\t    else:\n179\t        raise TypeError(\n180\t            f\"Response must be response-like (with status_code attribute) or tuple (status, body), \"\n181\t            f\"got {type(response).__name__}\"\n182\t        )\n183\t\n184\t    # DESIGN DECISION: Normalize expected_status to a list for uniform checking\n185\t    # - Why: Single values and lists can be processed with the same logic\n186\t    # - Benefit: Reduces code duplication and edge cases\n187\t    # - Pattern: Always convert to list, then use \"in\" operator for checking\n188\t    expected_codes: List[int]\n189\t    if isinstance(expected_status, int):\n190\t        expected_codes = [expected_status]\n191\t    elif isinstance(expected_status, list):\n192\t        expected_codes = expected_status\n193\t    else:\n194\t        raise TypeError(\n195\t            f\"expected_status must be int or List[int], \"\n196\t            f\"got {type(expected_status).__name__}\"\n197\t        )\n198\t\n199\t    # Validate all status codes are integers\n200\t    for code in expected_codes:\n201\t        if not isinstance(code, int):\n202\t            raise TypeError(\n203\t                f\"All status codes must be integers, got {type(code).__name__}\"\n204\t            )\n205\t\n206\t    # Check if actual status matches any expected code\n207\t    is_valid = actual_status in expected_codes\n208\t\n209\t    if not is_valid and throw_on_error:\n210\t        # Determine which single status to show in error (use first if list)\n211\t        error_expected = expected_status  # Keep original format for error message\n212\t        raise StatusValidationError(\n213\t            actual=actual_status,\n214\t            expected=error_expected,\n215\t            response_body=response_body,\n216\t            url=url\n217\t        )\n218\t\n219\t    return is_valid\n220\t\n221\t\n222\tdef validate_http_status_codes(\n223\t    *responses: Union[requests.Response, tuple],\n224\t    expected_status: Union[int, List[int]],\n225\t    throw_on_error: bool =     except StatusValidationError:\n255\t            if throw_on_error:\n256\t                raise\n257\t            results.append(False)\n258\t\n259\t    return results\n260\t\n261\t\n262\t# status code).\n288\t\n289\t    Accepts any status code in the 300-399 range.\n290\t\n291\t    Args:\n292\t        response: HTTP response object\n293\t        throw_on_error: If True, throws on expected_status=list(range(300, 400)),\n301\t        throw_on_error=throw_on_error\n302\t    )\n303\t\n304\t\n305\tdef validate_client_error(response: Union[requests.Response, tuple],\n306\t                         throw_on_error: bool = True) -> bool:\n307\t    \"\"\"\n308\t    Validate response indicates a client error (4xx status code).\n309\t\n310\t    Accepts any status code in the 400-499 range.\n311\t\n312\t    Args:\n313\t        response: HTTP response object\n314\t        throw_on_error: If True, throws on validation failure\n315\t\n316\t    Returns:\n317\t        bool: True if throw_on_error=throw_on_error\n323\t    )\n324\t\n325\t\n326\tdef validate_server_error(response: Union[requests.Response, tuple],\n327\t                         throw_on_error: bool = True) -> =============================================================================\n348\t# CONTENT-TYPE HEADER VALIDATION\n349\t# =============================================================================\n350\t\n351\tclass ContentTypeValidationError(AssertionError):\n352\t    \"\"\"\n353\t    Custom assertion error for content-type header validation failures.\n354\t\n355\t    Provides clear, formatted error messages that show:\n356\t    - The actual content-type received\n357\t    - The expected content-type(s)\n358\t    - The response URL (if available)\n359\t    \"\"\"\n360\t\n361\t    def __init__(self,\n362\t                 actual: str,\n363\t                 expected: Union[str, List[str]],\n364\t                 url: Optional[str] = None):\n365\t        \"\"\"\n366\t        Initialize a content-type validation error.\n367\t\n368\t        Args:\n369\t            actual: The actual content-type header value\n370\t            expected: The expected content-type or list of allowed types\n371\t            url: Optional URL that was requested\n372\t        \"\"\"\n373\t        self.actual = actual\n374\t        self.expected = expected\n375\t        self.url = url\n376\t\n377\t        # Build detailed error message\n378\t        msg_parts = []\n379\t\n380\t        if url:\n381\t            msg_parts.append(f\"URL: {url}\")\n382\t\n383\t        msg_parts.append(f\"Expected Content-Type: {self._format_expected()}\")\n384\t        msg_parts.append(f\"Actual Content-Type: {actual}\")\n385\t\n386\t        message = \"\\n  \".join(msg_parts)\n387\t        super().__init__(message)\n388\t\n389\t    def _format_expected(self) -> str:\n390\t        \"\"\"Format expected content-types for display.\"\"\"\n391\t        if isinstance(self.expected, list):\n392\t            if len(self.expected) == 1:\n393\t                return str(self.expected[0])\n394\t            elif len(self.expected) == 2:\n395\t                return f\"{self.expected[0]} or {self.expected[1]}\"\n396\t            else:\n397\t                return f\"one of {self.expected}\"\n398\t        else:\n399\t            return str(self.expected)\n400\t\n401\t\n402\tdef _normalize_content_type(content_type: Optional[str]) -> str:\n403\t    \"\"\"\n404\t    Normalize a content-type string for comparison.\n405\t\n406\t    DESIGN DECISION: Pattern-matching content type validation\n407\t    - Why: Real-world responses often include charset and other parameters\n408\t    - Benefit: Tests remain robust when servers add/modify parameters\n409\t    - Pattern: Normalize both sides to MIME type only, then compare\n410\t\n411\t    This function:\n412\t    - Converts to lowercase (case-insensitive comparison)\n413\t    - Trims whitespace (handles sloppy headers)\n414\t    - Extracts the MIME type without parameters (e.g., 'application/json; charset=utf-8' -> 'application/json')\n415\t\n416\t    Args:\n417\t        content_type: The content-type string to normalize (can be None)\n418\t\n419\t    Returns:\n420\t        str: Normalized content-type, or empty string if input is None\n421\t    \"\"\"\n422\t    if not content_type:\n423\t        return \"\"\n424\t\n425\t    # Convert to lowercase and trim whitespace\n426\t    # HTTP headers are case-insensitive, so we normalize to lowercase\n427\t    content_type = content_type.lower().strip()\n428\t\n429\t    # Extract only the MIME type (before semicolon if present)\n430\t    # This handles cases like 'application/json; charset=utf-8' -> 'application/json'\n431\t    # DESIGN DECISION: Ignore parameters for comparison\n432\t    # - Why: Tests should validate MIME type, not specific charset or encoding\n433\t    # - Benefit: Tests pass even when servers change charset parameters\n434\t    if ';' in content_type:\n435\t        content_type = content_type.split(';')[0].strip()\n436\t\n437\t    return content_type\n438\t\n439\t\n440\tdef validate_content_type(\n441\t    response: Union[requests.Response, tuple],\n442\t    expected_type: Union[str, List[str]],\n443\t    throw_on_error: bool = True\n444\t) -> bool:\n445\t    \"\"\"\n446\t    Validate HTTP response content-type header against expected value(s).\n447\t\n448\t    This helper function validates that an HTTP response has the expected content-type.\n449\t    It supports both single content-types and lists of allowed types, making it\n450\t    flexible for different testing scenarios. The function uses pattern matching,\n451\t    so 'application/json' will match 'application/json; charset=utf-8'.\n452\t\n453\t    Args:\n454\t        response: HTTP response object (requests.Response) or tuple of (status_code, headers, body)\n455\t        expected_type: Expected content-type (str) or list of allowed types (List[str])\n456\t        throw_on_error: If True, throws ContentTypeValidationError on validation failure.\n457\t                       If False, returns False instead.\n458\t\n459\t    Returns:\n460\t        bool: True if content-type matches expected value(s), False otherwise\n461\t\n462\t    Raises:\n463\t        ContentTypeValidationError: If validation fails and throw_on_error is True\n464\t        TypeError: If response or expected_type has invalid type\n465\t\n466\t    Examples:\n467\t        >>> # Single content-type validation\n468\t        >>> response = requests.get('http://example.com/api')\n469\t        >>> validate_content_type(response, 'application/json')\n470\t\n471\t        >>> # Multiple allowed content-types\n472\t        >>> validate_content_type(response, ['application/json', 'application/xml'])\n473\t\n474\t        >>> # Pattern matching (matches both 'application/json' and 'application/json; charset=utf-8')\n475\t        >>> validate_content_type(response, 'application/json')\n476\t\n477\t        >>> # Using tuple response format\n478\t        >>> status, headers, body = curl_request(...)\n479\t        >>> validate_content_type((status, headers, body), 'text/html')\n480\t\n481\t        >>> # Non-throwing validation\n482\t        >>> is_valid = validate_content_type(response, 'application/json', throw_on_error=False)\n483\t        >>> if is_valid:\n484\t        ...     print(\"Content-type is valid!\")\n485\t\n486\t    Acceptance Criteria:\n487\t    - Function accepts a response object and expected content-type(s)\n488\t    - Supports pattern matching (e.g., 'application/json' matches 'application/json; charset=utf-8')\n489\t    - Returns boolean or throws assertion error with clear message\n490\t    - Includes test cases demonstrating various content-type scenarios\n491\t    - Function is exported from test utils module\n492\t    \"\"\"\n493\t    # Extract content-type and URL from response\n494\t    actual_content_type: Optional[str] = None\n495\t    url: Optional[str] = None\n496\t\n497\t    # Check for response-like objects (duck typing)\n498\t    # First check if it's a tuple (special case)\n499\t    if isinstance(response, tuple) and len(response) >= 2:\n500\t        # Tuple format: (status_code, headers, body) or (status_code, headers)\n501\t        if len(response) >= 2:\n502\t            headers = response[1]\n503\t            if isinstance(headers, dict):\n504\t                actual_content_type = headers.get('Content-Type', headers.get('content-type'))\n505\t            elif isinstance(headers, str):\n506\t                # Headers might be a string in some test scenarios\n507\t                actual_content_type = headers\n508\t    # Then check if it has response-like attributes (headers, etc.)\n509\t    elif hasattr(response, 'headers'):\n510\t        actual_content_type = response.headers.get('Content-Type',\n511\t                                                     response.headers.get('content-type', ''))\n512\t        url = getattr(response, 'url', None)\n513\t    else:\n514\t        raise TypeError(\n515\t            f\"Response must be response-like (with headers attribute) or tuple (status, headers, body), \"\n516\t            f\"got {type(response).__name__}\"\n517\t        )\n518\t\n519\t    # Normalize the actual content-type for comparison\n520\t    normalized_actual = _normalize_content_type(actual_content_type)\n521\t\n522\t    # Normalize expected_type to a list for uniform checking\n523\t    expected_types: List[str]\n524\t    if isinstance(expected_type, str):\n525\t        expected_types = [expected_type]\n526\t    elif isinstance(expected_type, list):\n527\t        expected_types = expected_type\n528\t    else:\n529\t        raise TypeError(\n530\t            f\"expected_type must be str or List[str], \"\n531\t            f\"got {type(expected_type).__name__}\"\n532\t        )\n533\t\n534\t    # Validate all expected types are strings and normalize them\n535\t    normalized_expected = []\n536\t    for ct in expected_types:\n537\t        if not isinstance(ct, str):\n538\t            raise TypeError(\n539\t                f\"All content-types must be strings, got {type(ct).__name__}\"\n540\t            )\n541\t        normalized_expected.append(_normalize_content_type(ct))\n542\t\n543\t    # Check if actual content-type matches any expected type\n544\t    is_valid = normalized_actual in normalized_expected\n545\t\n546\t    if not is_valid and throw_on_error:\n547\t        # Use the original expected_type (not normalized) for error message\n548\t        error_expected = expected_type\n549\t        raise ContentTypeValidationError(\n550\t            actual=actual_content_type or \"(missing)\",\n551\t            expected=error_expected,\n552\t            url=url\n553\t        )\n554\t\n555\t    return is_valid\n556\t\n557\t\n558\tdef validate_json_content_type(\n559\t    response: Union[requests.Response, tuple],\n560\t    throw_on_error: bool = True\n561\t) -> bool:\n562\t    \"\"\"\n563\t    Validate response has JSON content-type.\n564\t\n565\t    Accepts any JSON content-type variant (application/json, text/json, etc.).\n566\t\n567\t    Args:\n568\t        response: HTTP response object\n569\t        throw_on_error: If True, throws on validation failure\n570\t\n571\t    Returns:\n572\t        bool: True if content-type indicates JSON\n573\t    \"\"\"\n574\t    json_types = [\n575\t        'application/json',\n576\t        'text/json',\n577\t        'application/vnd.api+json',\n578\t        'application/problem+json'\n579\t    ]\n580\t    return validate_content_type(\n581\t        response,\n582\t        expected_type=json_types,\n583\t        throw_on_error=throw_on_error\n584\t    )\n585\t\n586\t\n587\tdef       'text/xml',\n606\t        'application/vnd+xml',\n607\t        'application/rss+xml',\n608\t        'application/atom+xml'\n609\t    ]\n610\t    return validate_content_type(\n611\t        response,\n612\t        expected_type=xml_types,\n613\t        throw_on_error=throw_on_error\n614\t    )\n615\t\n616\t\n617\tdef validate_html_content_type(\n618\t    response: 'application/xhtml+xml'\n636\t    ]\n637\t    return validate_content_type(\n638\t        response,\n639\t        expected_type=html_types,\n640\t        throw_on_error=throw_on_error\n641\t    )\n642\t\n643\t\n644\tdef validate_text_content_type(\n645\t    response: Union[requests.Response, tuple],\n646\t    throw_on_error: bool = True\n647\t) -> bool:\n648\t    \"\"\"\n649\t    Validate throws on validation failure\n656\t\n657\t    Returns:\n658\t        bool: True if content-type indicates plain text\n659\t    \"\"\"\n660\t    return validate_content_type(\n661\t        response,\n662\t        expected_type='text/plain',\n663\t        throw_on_error=throw_on_error\n664\t    )\n665\t\n666\t\n667\t# =============================================================================\n668\t# CORS HEADER VALIDATION\n669\t# =============================================================================\n670\t\n671\tclass CORSValidationError(AssertionError):\n672\t    \"\"\"\n673\t    Custom assertion error for CORS header validation failures.\n674\t\n675\t    Provides clear, formatted error messages that show:\n676\t    - The actual CORS headers received\n677\t    - The expected CORS headers or origin values\n678\t    - The response URL (if available)\n679\t    \"\"\"\n680\t\n681\t    def __init__(self,\n682\t                 message: str,\n683\t                 actual_headers: Optional[Dict[str, str]] = None,\n684\t                 expected_origin: Optional[str] = None,\n685\t                 url: Optional[str] = None):\n686\t        \"\"\"\n687\t        Initialize a CORS validation error.\n688\t\n689\t        Args:\n690\t            message: The main error message\n691\t            actual_headers: The actual CORS headers received\n692\t            expected_origin: The expected origin value\n693\t            url: Optional URL that was requested\n694\t        \"\"\"\n695\t        self.actual_headers = actual_headers or {}\n696\t        self.expected_origin = expected_origin\n697\t        self.url = url\n698\t\n699\t        # Build detailed error message\n700\t        msg_parts = []\n701\t\n702\t        if url:\n703\t            msg_parts.append(f\"URL: {url}\")\n704\t\n705\t        msg_parts.append(message)\n706\t\n707\t        if expected_origin:\n708\t            msg_parts.append(f\"Expected origin: {expected_origin}\")\n709\t\n710\t        if actual_headers:\n711\t            msg_parts.append(\"Actual CORS headers:\")\n712\t            for header, value in actual_headers.items():\n713\t                msg_parts.append(f\"  {header}: {value}\")\n714\t\n715\t        super().__init__(\"\\n  \".join(msg_parts))\n716\t\n717\t\n718\tdef _extract_cors_headers(response: Union[requests.Response, tuple]) -> Dict[str, str]:\n719\t    \"\"\"\n720\t    Extract CORS headers from a response object.\n721\t\n722\t    Args:\n723\t        response: HTTP response object (requests.Response) or tuple of (status_code, headers, body)\n724\t\n725\t    Returns:\n726\t        Dict[str, str]: Dictionary of CORS headers found in the response\n727\t    \"\"\"\n728\t    cors_headers = {}\n729\t\n730\t    # Common CORS header names (both lowercase and titlecase)\n731\t    cors_header_names = [\n732\t        'access-control-allow-origin',\n733\t        'Access-Control-Allow-Origin',\n734\t        'access-control-allow-methods',\n735\t        'Access-Control-Allow-Methods',\n736\t        'access-control-allow-headers',\n737\t        'Access-Control-Allow-Headers',\n738\t        'access-control-allow-credentials',\n739\t        'Access-Control-Allow-Credentials',\n740\t        'access-control-expose-headers',\n741\t        'Access-Control-Expose-Headers',\n742\t        'access-control-max-age',\n743\t        'Access-Control-Max-Age',\n744\t    ]\n745\t\n746\t    # Check for response-like objects (duck typing)\n747\t    if isinstance(response, tuple) and len(response) >= 2:\n748\t        # Tuple format: (status_code, headers, body) or (status_code, headers)\n749\t        headers = response[1]\n750\t        if isinstance(headers, dict):\n751\t            for header_name in cors_header_names:\n752\t                if header_name in headers:\n753\t                    # Normalize to lowercase for consistency\n754\t                    normalized_name = header_name.lower()\n755\t                    cors_headers[normalized_name] = headers[header_name]\n756\t    elif hasattr(response, 'headers'):\n757\t        # requests.Response-like object\n758\t        for header_name in cors_header_names:\n759\t            if header_name in response.headers:\n760\t                normalized_name = header_name.lower()\n761\t                cors_headers[normalized_name] = response.headers[header_name]\n762\t\n763\t    return cors_headers\n764\t\n765\t\n766\tdef validate_cors_headers(\n767\t    response: Union[requests.Response, tuple],\n768\t    expected_origin: Optional[str] = None,\n769\t    allow_wildcard: bool = True,\n770\t    require_allow_origin: bool = True,\n771\t    throw_on_error: bool = True\n772\t) -> bool:\n773\t    \"\"\"\n774\t    Validate CORS headers in an HTTP response.\n775\t\n776\t    This helper function validates that an HTTP response has proper CORS headers.\n777\t    It supports checking for the presence of Access-Control-Allow-Origin, validating\n778\t    specific origin values, and distinguishing between wildcard (*) and specific origins.\n779\t\n780\t    Args:\n781\t        response: HTTP response object (requests.Response) or tuple of (status_code, headers, body)\n782\t        expected_origin: Optional specific origin value to validate (e.g., 'https://example.com')\n783\t                        If None, only checks for presence of CORS headers\n784\t        allow_wildcard: If True, accepts wildcard (*) as a valid origin\n785\t        require_allow_origin: If True, requires Access-Control-Allow-Origin header to be present\n786\t        throw_on_error: If True, throws CORSValidationError on validation failure.\n787\t                       If False, returns False instead.\n788\t\n789\t    Returns:\n790\t        bool: True if CORS headers are valid, False otherwise\n791\t\n792\t    Raises:\n793\t        CORSValidationError: If validation fails and throw_on_error is True\n794\t        TypeError: If response has invalid type\n795\t\n796\t    Examples:\n797\t        >>> # Check for any CORS headers present\n798\t        >>> response = requests.get('http://example.com/api')\n799\t        >>> validate_cors_headers(response)\n800\t\n801\t        >>> # Validate specific origin\n802\t        >>> validate_cors_headers(response, expected_origin='https://myapp.com')\n803\t\n804\t        >>> # Reject wildcard, require specific origin\n805\t        >>> validate_cors_headers(response, expected_origin='https://myapp.com', allow_wildcard=False)\n806\t\n807\t        >>> # Using tuple response format\n808\t        >>> status, headers, body = curl_request(...)\n809\t        >>> validate_cors_headers((status, headers, body), expected_origin='https://example.com')\n810\t\n811\t        >>> # Non-throwing validation\n812\t        >>> is_valid = validate_cors_headers(response, expected_origin='https://example.com', throw_on_error=False)\n813\t        >>> if is_valid:\n814\t        ...     print(\"CORS headers are valid!\")\n815\t\n816\t    Acceptance Criteria:\n817\t    - Function accepts a response object and validates required CORS headers\n818\t    - Checks for Access-Control-Allow-Origin header\n819\t    - Optionally validates specific origin values\n820\t    - Supports checking for wildcard vs specific origins\n821\t    - Returns boolean or throws assertion error with clear message\n822\t    - Includes test cases for CORS header presence and values\n823\t    - Function is exported from test utils module\n824\t    \"\"\"\n825\t    # Extract URL for error messages\n826\t    url: Optional[str] = None\n827\t    if hasattr(response, 'url'):\n828\t        url = getattr(response, 'url', None)\n829\t\n830\t    # Extract CORS headers from response\n831\t    cors_headers = _extract_cors_headers(response)\n832\t\n833\t    # Check if Access-Control-Allow-Origin is present when required\n834\t    if require_allow_origin:\n835\t        allow_origin = cors_headers.get('access-control-allow-origin')\n836\t        if not allow_origin:\n837\t            if throw_on_error:\n838\t                raise CORSValidationError(\n839\t                    message=\"Missing required CORS header: Access-Control-Allow-Origin\",\n840\t                    actual_headers=cors_headers,\n841\t                    url=url\n842\t                )\n843\t            return False\n844\t\n845\t    # If no expected_origin is specified, just check for presence\n846\t    if expected_origin is None:\n847\t        # We already checked for presence above if require_allow_origin is True\n848\t        # If require_allow_origin is False and no expected_origin, we pass\n849\t        return True\n850\t\n851\t    # Validate the origin value\n852\t    allow_origin = cors_headers.get('access-control-allow-origin', '')\n853\t\n854\t    # Check for wildcard\n855\t    if allow_origin == '*':\n856\t        if allow_wildcard:\n857\t            return True\n858\t        else:\n859\t            if throw_on_error:\n860\t                raise CORSValidationError(\n861\t                    message=\"Wildcard origin (*) is not allowed when specific origin is expected\",\n862\t                    actual_headers=cors_headers,\n863\t                    expected_origin=expected_origin,\n864\t                    url=url\n865\t                )\n866\t            return False\n867\t\n868\t    # Check for exact origin match\n869\t    if allow_origin == expected_origin:\n870\t        return True\n871\t\n872\t    # Origin doesn't match\n873\t    if throw_on_error:\n874\t        raise CORSValidationError(\n875\t            message=f\"Origin mismatch: expected '{expected_origin}' but got '{allow_origin}'\",\n876\t            actual_headers=cors_headers,\n877\t            expected_origin=expected_origin,\n878\t            url=url\n879\t        )\n880\t    return False\n881\t\n882\t\n883\tdef validate_cors_allow_origin(\n884\t    response: Union[requests.Response, tuple],\n885\t    throw_on_error: bool = True\n886\t) -> bool:\n887\t    \"\"\"\n888\t    Validate response has Access-Control-Allow-Origin header.\n889\t\n890\t    This is a convenience function that only checks for presence of the header,\n891\t    regardless of its value.\n892\t\n893\t    Args:\n894\t        response: HTTP response object\n895\t        throw_on_error: If True, throws on validation failure\n896\t\n897\t    Returns:\n898\t make_http_request(\n1025\t    base_url: str,\n1026\t    path: str = \"\",\n1027\t    method: str = \"GET\",\n1028\t    headers: Optional[Dict[str, str]] = None,\n1029\t    body: Optional[Union[str, The actual status code received\n    - The expected status code(s)\n    - The response body (truncated if too long)\n    \"\"\"\n\n    def __init__(self,\n                 actual: int,\n                 expected: Union[int, List[int]],\n                 response_body: Optional[str] = None,\n                 url: Optional[str] = None):\n        \"\"\"\n        Initialize a status validation error.\n\n        Args:\n            actual: The actual HTTP status code received\n            expected: The expected status code or list of allowed codes\n            response_body: Optional response body for debugging\n            url: Optional URL that was requested\n        \"\"\"\n        self.actual = actual\n        self.expected = expected\n        self.response_body = response_body\n        self.url = url\n\n        # Build detailed error message\n        msg_parts = []\n\n        if url:\n            msg_parts.append(f\"URL: {url}\")\n\n        msg_parts.append(f\"Expected HTTP status code: {self._format_expected()}\")\n        msg_parts.append(f\"Actual status code: {actual}\")\n\n        if response_body:\n            # Truncate response body if too long\n            body_preview = response_body[:200]\n            if len(response_body) > 200:\n                body_preview += \"... (truncated)\"\n            msg_parts.append(f\"Response body: {body_preview}\")\n\n        message = \"\\n  \".join(msg_parts)\n        super().__init__(message)\n\n    def _format_expected(self) -> str:\n        \"\"\"Format expected status codes for display.\"\"\"\n        if isinstance(self.expected, list):\n            if len(self.expected) == 1:\n                return str(self.expected[0])\n            elif len(self.expected) == 2:\n                return f\"{self.expected[0]} or {self.expected[1]}\"\n            else:\n                return f\"one of {self.expected}\"\n        else:\n            return str(self.expected)\n\n\ndef validate_http_status(\n    response: Union[requests.Response, tuple],\n    expected_status: Union[int, List[int]],\n    throw_on_error: bool = True\n) -> bool:\n    \"\"\"\n    Validate HTTP response status code against expected value(s).\n\n    This helper function validates that an HTTP response has the expected status code.\n    It supports both single status codes and lists of allowed codes, making it\n    flexible for different testing scenarios (e.g., accepting both 200 and 204).\n\n    Args:\n        response: HTTP response object (requests.Response) or tuple of (status_code, body)\n        expected_status: Expected HTTP status code (int) or list of allowed codes (List[int])\n        throw_on_error: If True, throws StatusValidationError on validation failure.\n                       If False, returns False instead.\n\n    Returns:\n        bool: True if status code matches expected value(s), False otherwise\n\n    Raises:\n        StatusValidationError: If validation fails and throw_on_error is True\n        TypeError: If response or expected_status has invalid type\n\n    Examples:\n        >>> # Single status code validation\n        >>> response = requests.get('http://example.com')\n        >>> validate_http_status(response, 200)\n\n        >>> # Multiple allowed status codes\n        >>> validate_http_status(response, [200, 201, 204])\n\n        >>> # Using tuple response format (from curl-based tests)\n        >>> status, body = curl_request(...)\n    ...     print(\"Status code is valid!\")\n\n    Acceptance Criteria:\n    - Function accepts a response object and expected status code(s)\n    - Supports both single status codes and arrays of module\n    \"\"\"\n    # Extract status code and body from response\n    actual_status: int\n    response_body: Optional[str] = None\n    url: Optional[str] = None\n\n    # DESIGN DECISION: Use duck typing for response handling\n    # - Why: Support both requests.Response objects and simple tuples\n    # - Benefit: Tests can work without external dependencies\n    # - Pattern: Check tuple first (special case), then attributes (duck typing)\n\n    # First check if it's a tuple (special case)\n    # Tuples are checked before hasattr() because they don't have status_code attribute\n    # Tuple format: (status_code,) or (status_code, body) or (status_code, headers, body)\n    if isinstance(response, tuple) and len(response) >= 1:\n        actual_status = response[0]\n        if len(response) > 1:\n            response_body = str(response[1])\n    # Then check if it has response-like attributes (status_code, etc.)\n    # This handles requests.Response objects and any similar response-like objects\n    elif hasattr(response, 'status_code'):\n        actual_status = response.status_code\n        response_body = getattr(response, 'text', None)\n        url = getattr(response, 'url', None)\n    else:\n        raise TypeError(\n            f\"Response must be response-like (with status_code attribute) or tuple (status, body), \"\n            f\"got {type(response).__name__}\"\n        )\n\n    # DESIGN DECISION: Normalize expected_status to a list for uniform checking\n    # - Why: Single values and lists can be processed with the same logic\n    # - Benefit: Reduces code duplication and edge cases\n    # - Pattern: Always convert to list, then use \"in\" operator for checking\n    expected_codes: List[int]\n    if isinstance(expected_status, int):\n        expected_codes = [expected_status]\n    elif isinstance(expected_status, list):\n        expected_codes = expected_status\n    else:\n        raise TypeError(\n            f\"expected_status must be int or List[int], \"\n            f\"got {type(expected_status).__name__}\"\n        )\n\n    # Validate all status codes are integers\n    for code in expected_codes:\n        if not isinstance(code, int):\n            raise TypeError(\n                f\"All status codes must be integers, got {type(code).__name__}\"\n            )\n\n    # Check if actual status matches any expected code\n    is_valid = actual_status in expected_codes\n\n    if not is_valid and throw_on_error:\n        # Determine which single status to show in error (use first if list)\n        error_expected = expected_status  # Keep original format for error message\n        raise StatusValidationError(\n            actual=actual_status,\n            expected=error_expected,\n            response_body=response_body,\n            url=url\n        )\n\n    return is_valid\n\n\ndef validate_http_status_codes(\n    *responses: Union[requests.Response, tuple],\n    expected_status:       response,\n        expected_status=list(range(500, 600)),\n        throw_on_error=throw_on_error\n    )\n\n\n# =============================================================================\n# CONTENT-TYPE HEADER VALIDATION\n# =============================================================================\n\nclass ContentTypeValidationError(AssertionError):\n    \"\"\"\n    Custom assertion error for content-type header validation failures.\n\n    Provides clear, formatted error messages that show:\n    - The actual content-type received\n    - The expected content-type(s)\n    - The response URL (if available)\n    \"\"\"\n\n    def __init__(self,\n                 actual: str,\n                 expected: Union[str, List[str]],\n                 url: Optional[str] = None):\n        \"\"\"\n        Initialize a content-type validation error.\n\n        Args:\n            actual: The actual content-type header value\n            expected: The expected content-type or list of allowed types\n            url: Optional URL that was requested\n        \"\"\"\n        self.actual = actual\n        self.expected = expected\n        self.url = url\n\n        # Build detailed error message\n        msg_parts = []\n\n        if url:\n            msg_parts.append(f\"URL: {url}\")\n\n        msg_parts.append(f\"Expected Content-Type: {self._format_expected()}\")\n        msg_parts.append(f\"Actual Content-Type: {actual}\")\n\n        message = \"\\n  \".join(msg_parts)\n        super().__init__(message)\n\n    def _format_expected(self) -> str:\n        \"\"\"Format expected content-types for display.\"\"\"\n        if isinstance(self.expected, list):\n            if len(self.expected) == 1:\n                return str(self.expected[0])\n            elif len(self.expected) == 2:\n                return f\"{self.expected[0]} or {self.expected[1]}\"\n            else:\n                return f\"one of {self.expected}\"\n        else:\n            return str(self.expected)\n\n\ndef _normalize_content_type(content_type: Optional[str]) -> str:\n    \"\"\"\n    Normalize a content-type string for comparison.\n\n    DESIGN DECISION: Pattern-matching content type validation\n    - Why: Real-world responses often include charset and other parameters\n    - Benefit: Tests remain robust when servers add/modify parameters\n    - Pattern: Normalize both sides to MIME type only, then compare\n\n    This function:\n    - Converts to lowercase (case-insensitive comparison)\n    - Trims whitespace (handles sloppy headers)\n    - Extracts the MIME type without parameters (e.g., 'application/json; charset=utf-8' -> 'application/json')\n\n    Args:\n        content_type: The content-type string to normalize (can be None)\n\n    Returns:\n        str: Normalized content-type, or empty string if input is None\n    \"\"\"\n    if not content_type:\n        return \"\"\n\n    # Convert to lowercase and trim whitespace\n    # HTTP headers are case-insensitive, so we normalize to lowercase\n    content_type = content_type.lower().strip()\n\n    # Extract only the MIME type (before semicolon if present)\n    # This handles cases like 'application/json; charset=utf-8' -> 'application/json'\n    # DESIGN DECISION: Ignore parameters for comparison\n    # - Why: Tests should validate MIME type, not specific charset or encoding\n    # - Benefit: Tests pass even when servers change charset parameters\n    if ';' in content_type:\n        content_type = content_type.split(';')[0].strip()\n\n    return content_type\n\n\ndef validate_content_type(\n    response: Union[requests.Response, tuple],\n    expected_type: Union[str, List[str]],\n    throw_on_error: bool = True\n) -> bool:\n    \"\"\"\n    Validate HTTP response content-type header against expected value(s).\n\n    This helper function validates that an HTTP response has the expected content-type.\n    It supports both single content-types and lists of allowed types, making it\n    flexible for different testing scenarios. The function uses pattern matching,\n    so 'application/json' will match 'application/json; charset=utf-8'.\n\n    Args:\n        response: HTTP response object (requests.Response) or tuple of (status_code, headers, body)\n        expected_type: Expected content-type (str) or list of allowed types (List[str])\n        throw_on_error: If True, throws ContentTypeValidationError on validation failure.\n                       If False, returns False instead.\n\n    Returns:\n        bool: True if content-type matches expected value(s), False otherwise\n\n    Raises:\n        ContentTypeValidationError: If validation fails and throw_on_error is True\n        TypeError: If response or expected_type has invalid type\n\n    Examples:\n        >>> # Single content-type validation\n        >>> response = requests.get('http://example.com/api')\n        >>> validate_content_type(response, 'application/json')\n\n        >>> # Multiple allowed content-types\n        >>> validate_content_type(response, ['application/json', 'application/xml'])\n\n        >>> # Pattern matching (matches both 'application/json' and 'application/json; charset=utf-8')\n        >>> validate_content_type(response, 'application/json')\n\n        >>> # Using tuple response format\n        >>> status, headers, body = curl_request(...)\n        >>> validate_content_type((status, headers, body), 'text/html')\n\n        >>> # Non-throwing validation\n        >>> is_valid = validate_content_type(response, 'application/json', throw_on_error=False)\n        >>> if is_valid:\n        ...     print(\"Content-type is valid!\")\n\n    Acceptance Criteria:\n    - Function accepts a response object and expected content-type(s)\n    - Supports pattern matching (e.g., 'application/json' matches 'application/json; charset=utf-8')\n    - Returns boolean or throws assertion error with clear message\n    - Includes test cases demonstrating various content-type scenarios\n    - Function is exported from test utils module\n    \"\"\"\n    # Extract content-type and URL from response\n    actual_content_type: Optional[str] = None\n    url: Optional[str] = None\n\n    # Check for response-like objects (duck typing)\n    # First check if it's a tuple (special case)\n    if isinstance(response, tuple) and len(response) >= 2:\n        # Tuple format: (status_code, headers, body) or (status_code, headers)\n        if len(response) >= 2:\n            headers = response[1]\n            if isinstance(headers, dict):\n                actual_content_type = headers.get('Content-Type', headers.get('content-type'))\n            elif isinstance(headers, str):\n                # Headers might be a string in some test scenarios\n                actual_content_type = headers\n    # Then check if it has response-like attributes (headers, etc.)\n    elif hasattr(response, 'headers'):\n        actual_content_type = response.headers.get('Content-Type',\n                                                     response.headers.get('content-type', ''))\n        url = getattr(response, 'url', None)\n    else:\n        raise TypeError(\n            f\"Response must be response-like (with headers attribute) or tuple (status, headers, body), \"\n            f\"got {type(response).__name__}\"\n        )\n\n    # Normalize the actual content-type for comparison\n    normalized_actual = _normalize_content_type(actual_content_type)\n\n    # Normalize expected_type to a list for uniform checking\n    expected_types: List[str]\n    if isinstance(expected_type, str):\n        expected_types = [expected_type]\n    elif isinstance(expected_type, list):\n        expected_types = expected_type\n    else:\n        raise TypeError(\n            f\"expected_type must be str or List[str], \"\n            f\"got {type(expected_type).__name__}\"\n        )\n\n    # Validate all expected types are strings and normalize them\n    normalized_expected = []\n    for ct in expected_types:\n        if not isinstance(ct, str):\n            raise TypeError(\n                f\"All content-types must be strings, got {type(ct).__name__}\"\n            )\n        normalized_expected.append(_normalize_content_type(ct))\n\n    # Check if actual content-type matches any expected type\n    is_valid = normalized_actual in normalized_expected\n\n    if not is_valid and throw_on_error:\n        # Use the original expected_type (not normalized) for error message\n        error_expected = expected_type\n        raise ContentTypeValidationError(\n            actual=actual_content_type or \"(missing)\",\n            expected=error_expected,\n            url=url\n        )\n\n    return is_valid\n\n\ndef validate_json_content_type(\n    response: Union[requests.Response, tuple],\n    throw_on_error: bool = True\n) -> bool:\n    \"\"\"\n    Validate response has JSON content-type.\n\n    Accepts any JSON content-type variant (application/json, text/json, etc.).\n\n    Args:\n        response: HTTP response object\n        throw_on_error: If True, throws on validation failure\n\n    Returns:\n        bool: True if content-type indicates JSON\n    \"\"\"\n    json_types = [\n        'application/json',\n        'text/json',\n        'application/vnd.api+json',\n        'application/problem+json'\n    ]\n    return validate_content_type(\n        response,\n        expected_type=json_types,\n        throw_on_error=throw_on_error\n    )\n\n\ndef validate_xml_content_type(\n    response: Union[requests.Response, tuple],\n    throw_on_error: bool = True\n) -> bool:\n    \"\"\"\n    Validate response has XML content-type.\n\n    Accepts any XML content-type variant (application/xml, text/xml, etc.).\n\n    Args:\n        response: HTTP response object\n        throw_on_error: If True, throws on validation failure\n\n    Returns:\n        bool: True if content-type indicates XML\n    \"\"\"\n    xml_types = [\n        'application/xml',\n        'text/xml',\n        'application/vnd+xml',\n        'application/rss+xml',\n        'application/atom+xml'\n    ]\n    return validate_content_type(\n        response,\n        expected_type=xml_types,\n        throw_on_error=throw_on_error\n    )\n\n\ndef validate_html_content_type(\n    response: Union[requests.Response, tuple],\n    throw_on_error: bool = True\n) -> bool:\n    \"\"\"\n    Validate response has HTML content-type.\n\n    Accepts any HTML content-type variant (text/html, application/xhtml+xml, etc.).\n\n    Args:\n        response: HTTP response object\n        throw_on_error: If True, throws on validation failure\n\n    Returns:\n        bool: True if content-type indicates HTML\n    \"\"\"\n    html_types = [\n        'text/html',\n        'application/xhtml+xml'\n    ]\n    return validate_content_type(\n        response,\n        expected_type=html_types,\n        throw_on_error=throw_on_error\n    )\n\n\ndef validate_text_content_type(\n    response: Union[requests.Response, tuple],\n    throw_on_error: bool = True\n) -> bool:\n    \"\"\"\n    Validate response has plain text content-type.\n\n    Accepts text/plain content-type.\n\n    Args:\n        response: HTTP response object\n        throw_on_error: If True, throws on validation failure\n\n    Returns:\n        bool: True if content-type indicates plain text\n    \"\"\"\n    return validate_content_type(\n        response,\n        expected_type='text/plain',\n        throw_on_error=throw_on_error\n    )\n\n\n# =============================================================================\n# CORS HEADER VALIDATION\n# =============================================================================\n\nclass CORSValidationError(AssertionError):\n    \"\"\"\n    Custom assertion error for CORS header validation failures.\n\n    Provides clear, formatted error messages that show:\n    - The actual CORS headers received\n    - The expected CORS headers or origin values\n    - The response URL (if available)\n    \"\"\"\n\n    def __init__(self,\n                 message: str,\n                 actual_headers: Optional[Dict[str, str]] = None,\n                 expected_origin: Optional[str] = None,\n                 url: Optional[str] = None):\n        \"\"\"\n        Initialize a CORS validation error.\n\n        Args:\n            message: The main error message\n            actual_headers: The actual CORS headers received\n            expected_origin: The expected origin value\n            url: Optional URL that was requested\n        \"\"\"\n        self.actual_headers = actual_headers or {}\n        self.expected_origin = expected_origin\n        self.url = url\n\n        # Build detailed error message\n        msg_parts = []\n\n        if url:\n            msg_parts.append(f\"URL: {url}\")\n\n        msg_parts.append(message)\n\n        if expected_origin:\n            msg_parts.append(f\"Expected origin: {expected_origin}\")\n\n        if actual_headers:\n            msg_parts.append(\"Actual CORS headers:\")\n            for header, value in actual_headers.items():\n                msg_parts.append(f\"  {header}: {value}\")\n\n        super().__init__(\"\\n  \".join(msg_parts))\n\n\ndef _extract_cors_headers(response: Union[requests.Response, tuple]) -> Dict[str, str]:\n    \"\"\"\n    Extract CORS headers from a response object.\n\n    Args:\n        response: HTTP response object (requests.Response) or tuple of (status_code, headers, body)\n\n    Returns:\n        Dict[str, str]: Dictionary of CORS headers found in the response\n    \"\"\"\n    cors_headers = {}\n\n    # Common CORS header names (both lowercase and titlecase)\n    cors_header_names = [\n        'access-control-allow-origin',\n        'Access-Control-Allow-Origin',\n        'access-control-allow-methods',\n        'Access-Control-Allow-Methods',\n        'access-control-allow-headers',\n        'Access-Control-Allow-Headers',\n        'access-control-allow-credentials',\n        'Access-Control-Allow-Credentials',\n        'access-control-expose-headers',\n        'Access-Control-Expose-Headers',\n        'access-control-max-age',\n        'Access-Control-Max-Age',\n    ]\n\n    # Check for response-like objects (duck typing)\n    if isinstance(response, tuple) and len(response) >= 2:\n        # Tuple format: (status_code, headers, body) or (status_code, headers)\n        headers = response[1]\n        if isinstance(headers, dict):\n            for header_name in cors_header_names:\n                if header_name in headers:\n                    # Normalize to lowercase for consistency\n                    normalized_name = header_name.lower()\n                    cors_headers[normalized_name] = headers[header_name]\n    elif hasattr(response, 'headers'):\n        # requests.Response-like object\n        for header_name in cors_header_names:\n            if header_name in response.headers:\n                normalized_name = header_name.lower()\n                cors_headers[normalized_name] = response.headers[header_name]\n\n    return cors_headers\n\n\ndef validate_cors_headers(\n    response: Union[requests.Response, tuple],\n    expected_origin: Optional[str] = None,\n    allow_wildcard: bool = True,\n    require_allow_origin: bool = True,\n    throw_on_error: bool = True\n) -> bool:\n    \"\"\"\n    Validate CORS headers in an HTTP response.\n\n    This helper function validates that an HTTP response has proper CORS headers.\n    It supports checking for the presence of Access-Control-Allow-Origin, validating\n    specific origin values, and distinguishing between wildcard (*) and specific origins.\n\n    Args:\n        response: HTTP response object (requests.Response) or tuple of (status_code, headers, body)\n        expected_origin: Optional specific origin value to validate (e.g., 'https://example.com')\n                        If None, only checks for presence of CORS headers\n        allow_wildcard: If True, accepts wildcard (*) as a valid origin\n        require_allow_origin: If True, requires Access-Control-Allow-Origin header to be present\n        throw_on_error: If True, throws CORSValidationError on validation failure.\n                       If False, returns False instead.\n\n    Returns:\n        bool: True if CORS headers are valid, False otherwise\n\n    Raises:\n        CORSValidationError: If validation fails and throw_on_error is True\n        TypeError: If response has invalid type\n\n    Examples:\n        >>> # Check for any CORS headers present\n        >>> response = requests.get('http://example.com/api')\n        >>> validate_cors_headers(response)\n\n        >>> # Validate specific origin\n        >>> validate_cors_headers(response, expected_origin='https://myapp.com')\n\n        >>> # Reject wildcard, require specific origin\n        >>> validate_cors_headers(response, expected_origin='https://myapp.com', allow_wildcard=False)\n\n        >>> # Using tuple response format\n        >>> status, headers, body = curl_request(...)\n        >>> validate_cors_headers((status, headers, body), expected_origin='https://example.com')\n\n        >>> # Non-throwing validation\n        >>> is_valid = validate_cors_headers(response, expected_origin='https://example.com', throw_on_error=False)\n        >>> if is_valid:\n        ...     print(\"CORS headers are valid!\")\n\n    Acceptance Criteria:\n    - Function accepts a response object and validates required CORS headers\n    - Checks for Access-Control-Allow-Origin header\n    - Optionally validates specific origin values\n    - Supports checking for wildcard vs specific origins\n    - Returns boolean or throws assertion error with clear message\n    - Includes test cases for CORS header presence and values\n    - Function is exported from test utils module\n    \"\"\"\n    # Extract URL for error messages\n    url: Optional[str] = None\n    if hasattr(response, 'url'):\n        url = getattr(response, 'url', None)\n\n    # Extract CORS headers from response\n    cors_headers = _extract_cors_headers(response)\n\n    # Check if Access-Control-Allow-Origin is present when required\n    if require_allow_origin:\n        allow_origin = cors_headers.get('access-control-allow-origin')\n        if not allow_origin:\n            if throw_on_error:\n                raise CORSValidationError(\n                    message=\"Missing required CORS header: Access-Control-Allow-Origin\",\n                    actual_headers=cors_headers,\n                    url=url\n                )\n            return False\n\n    # If no expected_origin is specified, just check for presence\n    if expected_origin is None:\n        # We already checked for presence above if require_allow_origin is True\n        # If require_allow_origin is False and no expected_origin, we pass\n        return True\n\n    # Validate the origin value\n    allow_origin = cors_headers.get('access-control-allow-origin', '')\n\n    # Check for wildcard\n    if allow_origin == '*':\n        if allow_wildcard:\n            return True\n        else:\n            if throw_on_error:\n                raise CORSValidationError(\n                    message=\"Wildcard origin (*) is not allowed when specific origin is expected\",\n                    actual_headers=cors_headers,\n                    expected_origin=expected_origin,\n                    url=url\n                )\n            return False\n\n    # Check for exact origin match\n    if allow_origin == expected_origin:\n        return True\n\n    # Origin doesn't match\n    if throw_on_error:\n        raise CORSValidationError(\n            message=f\"Origin mismatch: expected '{expected_origin}' but got '{allow_origin}'\",\n            actual_headers=cors_headers,\n            expected_origin=expected_origin,\n            url=url\n        )\n    return False\n\n\ndef validate_cors_allow_origin(\n    response: Union[requests.Response, tuple],\n    throw_on_error: bool = True\n) -> bool:\n    \"\"\"\n    Validate response has Access-Control-Allow-Origin header.\n\n    This is a convenience function that only checks for presence of the header,\n    regardless of its value.\n\n    Args:\n        response: HTTP response object\n        throw_on_error: If True, throws on validation failure\n\n    Returns:\n        bool: True if Access-Control-Allow-Origin header is present\n    \"\"\"\n    return validate_cors_headers(\n        response,\n        require_allow_origin=True,\n        throw_on_error=throw_on_error\n    )\n\n\ndef validate_cors_wildcard(\n    response: Union[requests.Response, tuple],\n    throw_on_error: bool = True\n) -> bool:\n    \"\"\"\n    Validate response has wildcard CORS origin.\n\n    Checks that Access-Control-Allow-Origin is set to '*'.\n\n    Args:\n        response: HTTP response object\n        throw_on_error: If True, throws on validation failure\n\n    Returns:\n        bool: True if CORS allows any origin\n    \"\"\"\n    return validate_cors_headers(\n        response,\n        expected_origin='*',\n        allow_wildcard=True,\n        throw_on_error=throw_on_error\n    )\n\n\ndef validate_cors_specific_origin(\n    response: Union[requests.Response, tuple],\n    origin: str,\n    throw_on_error: bool = True\n) -> bool:\n    \"\"\"\n    Validate response has specific CORS origin.\n\n    Checks that Access-Control-Allow-Origin matches the given origin exactly.\n    Rejects wildcard unless the wildcard is the expected origin.\n\n    Args:\n        response: HTTP response object\n        origin: The expected origin value (e.g., 'https://example.com')\n        throw_on_error: If True, throws on validation failure\n\n    Returns:\n        bool: True if CORS allows the specific origin\n    \"\"\"\n    return validate_cors_headers(\n        response,\n        expected_origin=origin,\n        allow_wildcard=(origin == '*'),  # Only allow wildcard if it's the expected value\n        throw_on_error=throw_on_error\n    )\n\n\ndef validate_cors_credentials(\n    response: Union[requests.Response, tuple],\n    throw_on_error: bool = True\n) -> bool:\n    \"\"\"\n    Validate response has Access-Control-Allow-Credentials header.\n\n    Checks for presence of Access-Control-Allow-Credentials header.\n    Useful for APIs that support cookies or authentication.\n\n    Args:\n        response: HTTP response object\n        throw_on_error: If True, throws on validation failure\n\n    Returns:\n        bool: True if Access-Control-Allow-Credentials header is present\n    \"\"\"\n    cors_headers = _extract_cors_headers(response)\n\n    allow_creds = cors_headers.get('access-control-allow-credentials')\n    if not allow_creds:\n        if throw_on_error:\n            url = getattr(response, 'url', None) if hasattr(response, 'url') else None\n            raise CORSValidationError(\n                message=\"Missing required CORS header: Access-Control-Allow-Credentials\",\n                actual_headers=cors_headers,\n                url=url\n            )\n        return False\n\n    return True\n\n\n# =============================================================================\n# HTTP REQUEST HELPER\n# =============================================================================\n\nclass HTTPRequestError(Exception):\n    \"\"\"\n    Custom exception for HTTP request failures.\n\n    Provides clear error messages that include:\n    - The HTTP method and URL\n    - The error type (connection, timeout, etc.)\n    - The underlying error message\n    \"\"\"\n\n    def __init__(self, method: str, url: str, error_type: str, message: str):\n        \"\"\"\n        Initialize an HTTP request error.\n\n        Args:\n            method: The HTTP method used (GET, POST, etc.)\n            url: The URL that was requested\n            error_type: Type of error (connection, timeout, etc.)\n            message: Detailed error message\n        \"\"\"\n        self.method = method\n        self.url = url\n        self.error_type = error_type\n\n        super().__init__(\n            f\"HTTP {method} request to {url} failed: {error_type}\\n  {message}\"\n        )\n\n\ndef make_http_request(\n    base_url: str,\n    path: str = \"\",\n    method: str = \"GET\",\n    headers: Optional[Dict[str, str]] = None,\n    body: Optional[Union[str, Dict[str, Any], bytes]] = None,\n    timeout: int = 30,\n    raise_on_error: bool = False\n) -> Dict[str, Any]:\n    \"\"\"\n    Make an HTTP request to a test server.\n\n    This helper function provides a simple interface for making HTTP requests\n    to a test server. It supports all common HTTP methods, custom headers,\n    request bodies, and graceful error handling.\n\n    Args:\n        base_url: The base URL of the test server (e.g., 'http://localhost:8080')\n        path: Optional path to append to the base URL (e.g., '/api/users')\n        method: HTTP method (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, PATCH, HEAD, OPTIONS). Defaults to 'GET'.\n        headers: Optional dictionary of HTTP headers to include in the request\n        body: Optional request body (string, dict for JSON, or bytes)\n        timeout: Request timeout in seconds (default: 30)\n        raise_on_error: If True, raises HTTPRequestError on connection errors.\n                       If False, returns error info in the response dict.\n\n    Returns:\n        Dict[str, Any]: A dictionary containing:\n            - 'success': bool - True if request succeeded, False on error\n            - 'status_code': int - HTTP status code (0 on connection error)\n            - 'headers': Dict[str, str] - Response headers (empty on error)\n            - 'body': str - Response body as string (error message on connection error)\n            - 'url': str - The full URL that was requested\n            - 'method': str - The HTTP method used\n            - 'error': Optional[str] - Error type if connection failed (e.g., 'connection', 'timeout')\n\n    Raises:\n        HTTPRequestError: If raise_on_error is True and a connection error occurs\n        ValueError: If requests library is not available or invalid parameters\n\n    Examples:\n        >>> # Simple GET request\n        >>> response = make_http_request('http://localhost:8080', '/api/health')\n        >>> if response['success']:\n        ...     print(f\"Status: {response['status_code']}\")\n        ...     print(f\"Body: {response['body']}\")\n\n        >>> # POST request with JSON body\n        >>> import json\n        >>> data = {'name': 'test', 'value': 123}\n        >>> response = make_http_request(\n        ...     'http://localhost:8080',\n        ...     '/api/users',\n        ...     method='POST',\n        ...     headers={'Content-Type': 'application/json'},\n        ...     body=json.dumps(data)\n        ... )\n\n        >>> # PUT request with custom headers\n        >>> response = make_http_request(\n        ...     'http://localhost:8080',\n        ...     '/api/users/123',\n        ...     method='PUT',\n        ...     headers={'Authorization': 'Bearer token123'},\n        ...     body='{\"name\": \"updated\"}'\n        ... )\n\n        >>> # DELETE request\n        >>> response = make_http_request(\n        ...     'http://localhost:8080',\n        ...     '/api/users/123',\n        ...     method='DELETE'\n        ... )\n\n        >>> # With automatic JSON serialization (dict body)\n        >>> response = make_http_request(\n        ...     'http://localhost:8080',\n        ...     '/api/data',\n        ...     method='POST',\n        ...     body={'key': 'value'}  # Automatically serialized to JSON\n        ... )\n\n        >>> # With error handling\n        >>> try:\n        ...     response = make_http_request(\n        ...         'http://localhost:8080',\n        ...         '/api/test',\n        ...         raise_on_error=True\n        ...     )\n        ... except HTTPRequestError as e:\n        ...     print(f\"Request failed: {e}\")\n\n    Acceptance Criteria:\n    - Function makes HTTP requests to a test server ✓\n    - Supports configurable HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, PATCH) ✓\n    - Allows setting request headers and body ✓\n    - Returns the full response including status code, headers, and body ✓\n    - Handles connection errors gracefully ✓\n    - Includes basic usage examples in comments ✓\n    \"\"\"\n    # Check if requests library is available\n    if not REQUESTS_AVAILABLE:\n        raise ValueError(\n            \"The 'requests' library is required for make_http_request but is not installed. \"\n            \"Install it with: pip install requests\"\n        )\n\n    # Validate and normalize HTTP method\n    method_upper = method.upper()\n    valid_methods = {'GET', 'POST', 'PUT', 'DELETE', 'PATCH', 'HEAD', 'OPTIONS'}\n    if method_upper not in valid_methods:\n        raise ValueError(\n            f\"Invalid HTTP method '{method}'. Must be one of: {', '.join(sorted(valid_methods))}\"\n        )\n\n    # Construct full URL\n    url = base_url.rstrip('/')\n    if path:\n        url += '/' + path.lstrip('/')\n\n    # Prepare headers\n    request_headers = (headers or {}).copy()\n\n    # Prepare body\n    request_body = body\n    if isinstance(body, dict):\n        # Automatically serialize dict to JSON\n        import json\n        request_body = json.dumps(body)\n        # Set Content-Type if not already set\n        if 'Content-Type' not in request_headers and 'content-type' not in request_headers:\n {"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"call_6e3c73a94e234957836d05df","type":"tool_result","content":"1\t# ARMOR Test Infrastructure\n2\t\n3\t## Overview\n4\t\n5\tThis package provides a comprehensive test infrastructure for validating ARMOR's HTTP error responses and API behaviors. It includes reusable helpers, pre-configured test fixtures, and structured test tables that make writing robust tests faster and more consistent.\n6\t\n7\t## Architecture\n8\t\n9\tThe test infrastructure is organized into three main layers:\n10\t\n11\t### 1. **Validation Helpers** (`test_helpers.py`)\n12\t\n13\tLow-level validation functions for HTTP responses:\n14\t\n15\t- **Status Validation**: `validate_http_status()`, `validate_success()`, `validate_client_error()`, `validate_server_error()`\n16\t- **Content-Type Validation**: `validate_content_type()`, `validate_json_content_type()`, `validate_xml_content_type()`\n17\t- **CORS Header Validation**: `validate_cors_headers()`, `validate_cors_allow_origin()`, `validate_cors_specific_origin()`\n18\t- **HTTP Request Helper**: `make_http_request()` for making test requests with error handling\n19\t\n20\t### 2. **Error Response Fixtures** (`fixtures/error_scenarios.py`)\n21\t\n22\tPre-configured error response fixtures for common HTTP error scenarios:\n23\t\n24\t- **Client Errors (4xx)**: 400 Bad Request, 403 Forbidden, 404 Not Found, 405 Method Not Allowed, 415 Unsupported Media Type\n25\t- **Server Errors (5xx)**: 500 Internal Server Error, 502 Bad Gateway, 503 Service Unavailable\n26\t- **Generic Builders**: `create_error_response()`, `create_error_batch()` for custom scenarios\n27\t\n28\t### 3. **Test Tables** (`test_tables.py`)\n29\t\n30\tStructured test case management for data-driven testing:\n31\t\n32\t- **Pre-configured Tables**: Common error scenarios, authentication tests, validation tests\n33\t- **Table Builders**: `create_auth_test_table()`, `create_not_found_test_table()`, `create_rate_limit_test_table()`\n34\t- **Execution Helpers**: `run_test_case()`, `run_test_table()` for running tests\n35\t\n36\t## Key Design Decisions\n37\t\n38\t### Fixture Immutability\n39\t\n40\tError fixtures use immutable builder patterns:\n41\t\n42\t```python\n43\tfixture = not_found_fixture(path=\"/api/users\")\n44\t# Returns new fixture, doesn't modify original\n45\twith_path = fixture.with_path(\"/api/items\")\n46\t```\n47\t\n48\t**Why**: This prevents accidental state mutations in parallel tests and makes test behavior predictable.\n49\t\n50\t### Tuple-Based Response Format\n51\t\n52\tValidation functions accept both `requests.Response` objects and simple tuples:\n53\t\n54\t```python\n55\t# With requests.Response\n56\tvalidate_http_status(response, 200)\n57\t\n58\t# With tuple (status, headers, body)\n59\tvalidate_http_status((200, {'Content-Type': 'application/json'}, '{}'), 200)\n60\t```\n61\t\n62\t**Why**: Tuples work without the `requests` library, making tests runnable in minimal environments and easier to mock.\n63\t\n64\t### Pattern-Matching Content Types\n65\t\n66\tContent-Type validation uses pattern matching:\n67\t\n68\t```python\n69\t# Both of these match 'application/json'\n70\tvalidate_content_type(response, 'application/json')\n71\tvalidate_content_type(response, 'application/json; charset=utf-8')\n72\t```\n73\t\n74\t**Why**: Real-world responses often include charset parameters. Pattern matching makes tests more robust without complex logic.\n75\t\n76\t### Collection-Based Fixtures\n77\t\n78\tFixtures are organized in collections by category:\n79\t\n80\t```python\n81\tfrom tests.fixtures.error_scenarios import CLIENT_ERROR_FIXTURES, SERVER_ERROR_FIXTURES\n82\t```\n83\t\n84\t**Why**: Enables bulk testing against all client errors or all server errors without maintaining manual lists.\n85\t\n86\t## Quick Start Examples\n87\t\n88\t### Validating HTTP Status Codes\n89\t\n90\t```python\n91\tfrom tests.test_helpers import validate_http_status, validate_client_error\n92\t\n93\t# Single status code\n94\tvalidate_http_status(response, 200)\n95\t\n96\t# Multiple acceptable codes\n97\tvalidate_http_status(response, [200, 201, 204])\n98\t\n99\t# Check for client error\n100\tvalidate_client_error(response)\n101\t```\n102\t\n103\t### Using Error Fixtures\n104\t\n105\t```python\n106\tfrom tests.fixtures.error_scenarios import not_found_fixture, get_fixture\n107\t\n108\t# Create a 404 fixture\n109\tfixture = not_found_fixture(path=\"/api/users/123\")\n110\tassert fixture.status_code == 404\n111\t\n112\t# Get pre-configured fixture\n113\tfixture = get_fixture('404_not_found')\n114\tassert fixture.status_code == 404\n115\t```\n116\t\n117\t### Making Test Requests\n118\t\n119\t```python\n120\tfrom tests.test_helpers import make_http_request\n121\t\n122\tresponse = make_http_request(\n123\t    base_url='http://localhost:8080',\n124\t    path='/api/health',\n125\t    method='GET'\n126\t)\n127\t\n128\tif response['success']:\n129\t    print(f\"Status: {response['status_code']}\")\n130\t    print(f\"Body: {response['body']}\")\n131\t```\n132\t\n133\t## Testing Patterns\n134\t\n135\t### Pattern 1: Fixture-Based Testing\n136\t\n137\tUse pre-configured fixtures for standard error scenarios:\n138\t\n139\t```python\n140\tdef test_404_not_found():\n141\t    \"\"\"Test 404 Not Found response structure.\"\"\"\n142\t    fixture = not_found_fixture(path=\"/api/users/123\")\n143\t    \n144\t    validate_http_status(fixture.to_tuple(), 404)\n145\t    validate_json_content_type(fixture.to_tuple())\n146\t    validate_standard_error_response(fixture.body)\n147\t```\n148\t\n149\t### Pattern 2: Table-Based Testing\n150\t\n151\tUse test tables for data-driven scenarios:\n152\t\n153\t```python\n154\tdef test_not_found_multiple_paths():\n155\t    \"\"\"Test 404 response for multiple path types.\"\"\"\n156\t    table = create_not_found_test_table([\n157\t        TestCase(path=\"/api/users/999\", expected_code=\"not_found\"),\n158\t        TestCase(path=\"/api/items/abc\", expected_code=\"not_found\"),\n159\t        TestCase(path=\"/api/orders/xyz\", expected_code=\"not_found\"),\n160\t    ])\n161\t    \n162\t    results = run_test_table(table)\n163\t    assert all(result.passed for result in results)\n164\t```\n165\t\n166\t### Pattern 3: Helper-Based Validation\n167\t\n168\tUse validation helpers for custom test logic:\n169\t\n170\t```python\n171\tdef test_custom_error_response():\n172\t    \"\"\"Test custom error response with specific fields.\"\"\"\n173\t    response = make_http_request('http://localhost:8080', '/api/error')\n174\t    \n175\t    validate_http_status(response['status_code'], 418)\n176\t    validate_content_type(response['headers'], 'application/json')\n177\t    \n178\t    # Custom validation\n179\t    body = json.loads(response['body'])\n180\t    assert body['error'] == 'custom_error'\n181\t    assert 'details' in body\n182\t```\n183\t\n184\t## Adding New Error Test Cases\n185\t\n186\tWhen adding new error test cases, follow these steps:\n187\t\n188\t1. **Choose Your Approach**:\n189\t   - Use existing fixtures for standard errors (404, 500, etc.)\n190\t   - Create custom fixtures with `create_error_response()` for new scenarios\n191\t   - Use test tables for multiple similar scenarios\n192\t\n193\t2. **Write the Test**:\n194\t   ```python\n195\t   def test_my_new_scenario():\n196\t )\n202\t       \n203\t       validate_http_status(fixture.to_tuple(), 418)\n204\t       validate_standard_error_response(fixture.body)\n205\t   ```\n206\t\n207\t3. **Run and Verify**:\n208\t   ```bash\n209\t   pytest tests/test_my_new_scenario.py -v\n210\t   ```\n211\t\n212\tSee [TEST_PATTERNS.md](TEST_PATTERNS.md) for detailed guidance on adding new test cases.\n213\t\n214\t## Module        # Fixture exports\n229\t    ├── error_scenarios.py              # Error response fixtures\n230\t    └── README.md                       # Fixture documentation\n231\t```\n232\t\n233\t##
